title: Tiger's Nest at First Light
excerpt: What it's actually like to climb to Taktsang before sunrise — and why so many travellers say it changes them.
content: The path to Tiger's Nest begins in pine forest, switching back and forth as the valley falls away beneath you. We started in the dark with head torches; by the time we reached the prayer-wheel viewpoint, the sun was just touching the cliff face above us. The monastery itself, suspended over a 900-metre drop, is smaller in person than the photographs suggest — and somehow more astonishing. We spent an hour inside the inner shrines and walked back down in silence, the sound of bells carrying up from the valley.
author: Karma D.

title: Festival Season — When to Go and Why
excerpt: A traveller's guide to Bhutan's tshechus — from Paro's grand spring festival to the smaller, quieter village dances.
content: Tshechus are the great religious festivals of Bhutan — three to five days of masked dances performed in the courtyards of dzongs and temples. Paro Tshechu (spring) and Thimphu Tshechu (autumn) are the largest and most photographed, but the village tshechus — Wangdue, Jakar, Trashigang — are often quieter and feel more intimate. If you can plan around one, do; sitting on the dzong walls watching the dances unfold is one of the great Bhutan experiences.
author: Pema T.

title: Understanding Bhutan's Sustainable Development Fee
excerpt: A simple, honest explanation of the SDF — what it is, what it pays for, and why we think it's a good thing.
content: Bhutan's Sustainable Development Fee is the country's version of 'high value, low volume' tourism. It is currently US$100 per adult per night (with reductions for some markets and children). The fee is paid directly to the government and funds healthcare, education, environmental conservation and infrastructure — not tour operators. It is what allows Bhutan to remain Bhutan, and to limit the kind of mass tourism that has changed so many other Himalayan destinations.
author: Sonam W.
